description: You are a compliance officer who has been asked to perform a lightweight security-baseline scan of the workstation you are logged in to. Create a single JSON report that captures five specific findings. The finished report must be written to /home/user/compliance_audit/security_audit_report.json and it must be the only file inside the directory /home/user/compliance_audit when you are finished. REQUIRED FINDINGS TO CAPTURE 1. open_tcp_ports – the list of TCP ports that are in LISTEN state on the local machine at the moment you run the scan. 2. password_max_days – the value that represents the maximum password age (in days) as defined in /etc/login.defs. 3. system_users – every user account whose UID is < 1000 (these are normally the “system” or “privileged” accounts). For each of those accounts include: • username • uid • shell 4. world_writable_logs – any files under /var/log that have the world-writable bit set. Report them by their full absolute path. 5. sha256_passwd – the SHA-256 checksum of /etc/passwd (hex-encoded, all lowercase, 64 characters). OUTPUT FORMAT – THIS IS WHAT THE AUTOMATED TEST WILL CHECK The file /home/user/compliance_audit/security_audit_report.json must be valid JSON and must have EXACTLY the following six top-level keys, in this order: { "open_tcp_ports": [...], "password_max_days": 0, "system_users": [...], "world_writable_logs": [...], "sha256_passwd": "", "generated_at": "" } Detailed requirements for each key: • open_tcp_ports – JSON array of one or more integer values. Integers must be in the range 1-65535. Duplicate ports must NOT appear. • password_max_days – a single JSON integer. • system_users – JSON array of objects. Each object must have three keys: {"username": "name", "uid": 0, "shell": "/bin/bash"} The order of those keys inside the object is not important but all three MUST be present for every user. • world_writable_logs – JSON array of strings. Each string must be an absolute path that starts with “/var/log”. It is acceptable for this array to be empty (i.e. []). • sha256_passwd – a 64-character lowercase hexadecimal string that matches the regular expression ^[a-f0-9]{64}$. • generated_at – an RFC 3339 / ISO 8601 timestamp of when you produced the report, for example “2024-06-15T14:07:30Z”. Either the trailing “Z” or an explicit numeric offset (e.g., “+00:00”) is acceptable. OTHER STRICT REQUIREMENTS 1. The directory /home/user/compliance_audit must exist at the end of the task and be owned by the current unprivileged user. 2. No other files may be present in that directory besides the single JSON report. 3. The report file must be readable with permissions no more permissive than 0644. 4. Use no more than 10 separate shell commands in total to gather data and create the report. When everything is finished, you should be able to run: jq . /home/user/compliance_audit/security_audit_report.json and see a well-formed JSON document that satisfies all of the above rules.
truth:
EXPECTED LOCATION
/home/user/compliance_audit/security_audit_report.json

MANDATORY STRUCTURE (used by automated tests)
The JSON file must:
• Be syntactically valid.  
• Have exactly the six top-level keys listed below and in the listed order:
  1. open_tcp_ports          → JSON array, every element must be an integer (regex ^[0-9]+$). The array may be empty or have values 1-65535.  
  2. password_max_days       → JSON integer (regex ^[0-9]+$).  
  3. system_users            → JSON array of objects.  Each object must contain the three string keys “username”, “uid”, “shell”. “uid” must hold a JSON integer.  
  4. world_writable_logs     → JSON array of strings; every string must begin with “/var/log”.  
  5. sha256_passwd           → JSON string matching regex ^[a-f0-9]{64}$.  
  6. generated_at            → JSON string matching regex ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}(Z|[+-][0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2})$.

The automated test harness will:
• Verify that only the path /home/user/compliance_audit/security_audit_report.json exists inside /home/user/compliance_audit/.  
• Stat the file to confirm permissions ≤0644.  
• Parse the JSON and validate the presence and datatype/pattern of each key as described above.  
• It will NOT attempt to validate the actual factual content (e.g., which ports are open); only structure, data types, and patterns are enforced.

No other hidden sentinel files are required.
